name: Offline Data + Sync
description: 100% offline data persistence with automatic sync on reconnect — including real SQL running on WebAssembly inside a Web Worker.
longDescription: Two parallel implementations of the same problem — persisting data offline and syncing it once the connection returns — using IndexedDB and OPFS with wa-sqlite (real SQLite compiled to WebAssembly, running inside a Web Worker via asynchronous message passing). The queue of pending notes is resolved by the Service Worker's Background Sync API as soon as connectivity returns, even with the tab closed; the OPFS worker reconciles its own state once it receives the sync-completed notice. The Storage API exposes the real storage quota and protects data from cache eviction once persistence is granted.
tags: OPFS, IndexedDB, wa-sqlite, WebAssembly, Web Workers, Sync, Storage API
architecture: OPFS, SQL, WebAssembly, Web Worker, IndexedDB, Background Sync Queue
technologies: OPFS, wa-sqlite, Web Workers, IndexedDB, Storage API, Background Sync API
concepts: OPFS, SQL over WebAssembly, Worker thread isolation, Asynchronous worker messaging, Main thread, Secondary thread, Persistence, Sync queue
emptyStateHint: Add a new note by clicking the button above
flowStep1: 1. Create a note while the app is fully online.
flowStep2Prefix: 2. Switch the app to offline mode. You can check how in the
flowStep3: 3. Create a note while the app is fully offline. Notice it gets a different status.
flowStep4: 4. Bring the app back online and watch the notes get updated to the online status.
modalNotePlaceholder: Type the text of your new note
backgroundSyncUnsupportedMessage: Background Sync isn't supported in this browser (Firefox and Safari never implemented this API — a deliberate decision by those vendors). The note was saved locally with offline status, but automatic sync on reconnect only works in Chromium-based browsers.
opfsInitErrorMessage: Couldn't initialize the SQLite database in OPFS in this browser or context (e.g. private browsing restricting sandboxed storage).
opfsDesc1: OPFS (Origin Private File System) is a private, browser-level file system that lets web applications store and manipulate files directly on the user's device.
opfsDesc2: In this demo, OPFS doesn't store data as loose files: it hosts a real SQLite database, compiled to WebAssembly with wa-sqlite and running entirely inside a Web Worker — notes are read and written with actual SQL (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), not raw file reads and writes.
opfsDesc3: Background Sync lets you schedule a sync that the browser runs as soon as the internet connection returns — even if the tab is closed.
opfsFlowIntro: When these technologies work together, a PWA can keep creating and modifying data even offline, and resolve the pending sync as soon as the connection returns.
opfsHowItWorksIntro: In practice, the flow works like this:
opfsHowItWorksItem1: the user creates a note in the application;
opfsHowItWorksItem2: if there's no internet at that moment, the note is saved locally in the SQLite database inside OPFS, with an offline status;
opfsHowItWorksItem3: the browser registers a sync request (sync.register()) that the Service Worker runs as soon as the connection returns;
opfsHowItWorksItem4: once done, the Service Worker notifies the open application, which reconciles the OPFS notes' status from offline to online.
opfsUseCasesDesc: This is very useful in applications that work with large files or content editing, such as image editors, documents, videos, PDFs, or upload systems.
opfsValueNote: In practice, the user can keep working normally even without internet, and the sync is resolved automatically as soon as the connection comes back.
opfsHowToUseIntro: Below is a small note-taking demo built on OPFS + SQLite over WebAssembly and Background Sync. Feel free to try it out:
opfsMethod1: new Worker(...) — starts a dedicated Web Worker to run SQLite without blocking the main thread
opfsMethod2: SQLiteAsyncFactory() (wa-sqlite) — loads the SQLite WebAssembly binary inside the worker
opfsMethod3: sqlite3.vfs_register(OriginPrivateFileSystemVFS) — registers OPFS as SQLite's file system
opfsMethod4: sqlite3.open_v2() / exec() / run() — opens the database and runs real SQL (CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT)
opfsMethod5: worker.postMessage() / onmessage — exchanges asynchronous messages between the main thread and the worker, each identified by an id
opfsMethod6: registration.sync.register() — schedules a background sync for when the connection returns
opfsMethod7: self.addEventListener("sync", ...) (Service Worker) — processes the queue of pending notes as soon as the browser fires the sync
opfsMethod8: client.postMessage(SYNC_COMPLETED) — notifies the open application to reconcile the OPFS notes' status
idbDesc1: IndexedDB is a local browser database used to store structured information directly on the user's device.
idbDesc2: Background Sync is a feature that lets you schedule a sync the browser runs as soon as the internet connection returns — even if the tab is closed.
idbFlowIntro: When these two technologies work together, web applications can keep working offline and resolve the pending sync automatically as soon as the connection returns.
idbHowItWorksIntro: In practice, the flow works like this:
idbHowItWorksItem1: the user performs an action in the app, like sending a message or creating a note;
idbHowItWorksItem2: if there's no internet at that moment, the data is stored in IndexedDB with an offline status;
idbHowItWorksItem3: the browser registers a sync request (sync.register()) that the Service Worker runs as soon as the connection returns;
idbHowItWorksItem4: the Service Worker itself reads IndexedDB, updates the pending notes' status to online, and notifies the open application — all of this happens even with the tab closed.
idbUseCasesDesc: This makes for far more resilient experiences, avoiding data loss and improving how PWAs behave offline.
idbExampleNote: A simple example would be a notes or messaging app that keeps working without internet and syncs everything automatically once the connection returns.
idbHowToUseIntro: Below is a small note-taking demo built on IndexedDB + Background Sync. Feel free to try it out:
idbMethod1: indexedDB.open() — opens (or creates, via onupgradeneeded) the browser's local database
idbMethod2: db.createObjectStore() — creates the notes object store with indexes on text, timestamp, and status
idbMethod3: store.add() / getAll() / put() / delete() — write and read operations on the object store, inside a transaction
idbMethod4: registration.sync.register() — schedules a background sync for when the connection returns
idbMethod5: self.addEventListener("sync", ...) (Service Worker) — reads IndexedDB and updates the pending notes' status, even with the tab closed
idbMethod6: client.postMessage(SYNC_COMPLETED) — notifies any open tab to refresh the displayed note list
storageDesc1: The Storage API is a set of browser technologies used to store data locally on the user's device.
storageDesc2: In web applications and PWAs, it lets you save information without fully depending on the internet, improving performance, offline experience, and data persistence.
storageTechIntro: Some of the main storage technologies are:
storageTechIndexedDB: IndexedDB: a local browser database. Used to store large volumes of structured data, such as messages, products, users, or offline content.
storageTechCache: Cache Storage: a system used mainly by Service Workers to store application files such as HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images, and API responses. It helps make the application faster and functional offline.
storageTechOPFS: OPFS (Origin Private File System): a private file area for the application inside the browser. It lets you create, edit, and manipulate files in a way that's much closer to a traditional operating system — including hosting a real SQLite database, like in the demo above.
storageModernAppsNote: In practice, these technologies help modern web applications behave more and more like installed apps.
storageEvictionDesc: IndexedDB and OPFS work with no special permission — you can use them directly. By default, though, the browser can automatically delete this data when the device is low on disk space, prioritizing origins the user visits least. This is called storage eviction.
storagePersistDesc: The persistence permission (navigator.storage.persist()) only protects this data from eviction — the browser commits to not deleting it without an explicit user action. It doesn't change the available quota or unlock any new capability.
storageBrowserDiffDesc: One important detail: in Chrome, this permission shows no dialog at all. The browser silently decides whether to grant it based on internal criteria — such as the app being installed as a PWA or having push notifications enabled. In Firefox, a popup is shown asking the user to confirm.
storageHowToUseText: Grant persistent storage by clicking the button below:
storageDeniedMessage: The browser silently denied the permission. In the browser, this is decided automatically based on internal criteria — install the app as a PWA or enable push notifications to increase the chance of it being granted.
storageUnsupportedMessage: The Storage API isn't supported in this browser — quota can't be checked and persistent storage can't be requested.
storageMethod1: navigator.storage.persisted() — checks whether this origin already has persistent storage granted
storageMethod2: navigator.storage.persist() — asks the browser to protect this origin's data from storage eviction
storageMethod3: navigator.storage.estimate() — returns the total quota available and the space already used by the origin
